sanskrit_iast:
sarva-karmāṇi manasā sannyasyāste sukhaṁ vaśī |
nava-dvāre pure dehī naiva kurvan na kārayan || 13 ||

anvaya.meaning: in the city of nine gates

translations.verbatim_quote: When the embodied living being controls his nature and mentally renounces all actions, he resides happily in the city of nine gates [the material body], neither working nor causing work to be done.
When the embodied living being controls his nature and mentally renounces all actions, he resides happily in the city of nine gates [the material body], neither working nor causing work to be done.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1439 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The embodied soul lives in the city of nine gates. The activities of the body, or the figurative city of body, are conducted automatically by its particular modes of nature. The soul, although subjecting himself to the conditions of the body, can be beyond those conditions, if he so desires.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.13 Aste, he continues; sukham, happily; sannyasya, having given up; sarva-karmani, all actions-nitya, naimittika, kamya and nisiddha (prohibited actions); [See note on p. 128.-Tr.] manasa, mentally, through discriminating wisdom-i.e. having given up (all actions) by seeing inaction in action, etc.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-13-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the self-controlled (vaśī) embodied one (dehī), having mentally renounced (manasā sannyasya) all actions through discriminating wisdom — i.e., having seen inaction in action — dwells happily in the city of nine gates (the body of nine orifices: two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, anus, genital), neither acting nor causing-others-to-act. Shankara emphasises this is the established jñānin's state: the body acts, the senses act, but the embodied Self neither acts nor causes.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.13 The embodied self who is self-controlled, renounces all actions to the city of nine gates, i.e., the body with its sensory and motor functions which are nine in number. He discriminates that all actions are due to conjunction of the self with the body which is rooted in previous Karmas, and is…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-13-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the self-controlled embodied self renounces all actions to the city of nine gates — i.e., the body with its sensory and motor functions which are nine in number. He discriminates that all actions are due to the conjunction of the self with the body, which is rooted in previous karma, and dwells happily, neither acting nor causing others to act. For Ramanuja, the renunciation is the recognition that action belongs to the body, not to the self.

positions.rendering: in the city of nine gates [the material body]
Advaita: the body of nine orifices is the field; the embodied Self dwells in it without acting
Vishishtadvaita: the embodied self renounces all actions to the body, recognising they belong to body-not-self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both classical readings preserve the body=nine-gated-city image. Shankara emphasises the embodied Self's non-action; Ramanuja emphasises the renunciation-of-action-to-the-body via discrimination. The metaphysical scope (the nature of the embodied Self vs. the body) is sectarian; the structural image is shared.

literal_meaning: The self-controlled embodied one, having mentally renounced all actions, dwells happily in the city of nine gates, neither acting nor causing action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the metaphor of the body as the city of nine gates (nava-dvāra-pure: two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, anus, genital). The vaśī (self-controlled) dehī (embodied) mentally renounces all action and dwells happily within this city, neither acting nor causing others to act. The verse anchors the inner-renunciation reading: bodily action continues; the embodied Self has renounced it at the level of mind.
